questions.question: According to organizational research, approximately what percentage of valuable knowledge is tacit (gained through experience rather than formal training)?

options.text: About 30% of valuable knowledge is tacit
About 50% of valuable knowledge is tacit
About 70% of valuable knowledge is tacit
Nearly all (95%+) valuable knowledge comes from formal education

questions.explanation: Research suggests approximately 70% of valuable knowledge is tacit—gained through experience, trial-and-error, and context-specific learning rather than formal instruction. This means your lived experience contains significant expertise that can't be taught in classrooms alone. Formal credentials are valuable, but experiential knowledge often proves more practical for real-world problem-solving.

questions.question: What is the difference between raw experience and teachable wisdom according to the lesson?

options.text: There is no difference—all experience automatically becomes wisdom over time
Teachable wisdom requires reflection, pattern identification, principle extraction, and contextual framing
Only traumatic or extremely difficult experiences can become teachable wisdom
Teachable wisdom requires formal credentials or professional training to validate the experience

questions.explanation: Having experience isn't the same as having teachable wisdom. The transformation requires: (1) reflecting on what happened, (2) identifying patterns across multiple experiences, (3) extracting principles or lessons, (4) considering context (when does this apply?), and (5) framing for others (how would this help someone else?). This systematic reflection converts raw experience into shareable insights.

questions.question: According to research on narrative identity, what is the association between coherent life narratives and wellbeing?

options.text: There is no established relationship between life narratives and psychological functioning
Coherent life narratives and meaning-making are associated with better psychological functioning
Only people with trauma-free lives can develop coherent narratives
Creating life narratives causes worse mental health by dwelling on the past

questions.explanation: Narrative identity research (particularly McAdams' work) shows that coherent life stories and meaning-making are associated with better psychological functioning. More coherent and agentic narratives, and narratives containing redemptive meaning-making, correlate with better mental health. However, this is correlational research, and the lesson emphasizes that narrative exercises should be framed as reflection tools, not guarantees of healing.

questions.question: Why do many people underestimate the value of their experience-based wisdom?

options.text: Because most people genuinely don't have any valuable wisdom to share
Due to credential bias, the familiarity trap, comparison problems, and imposter syndrome
Because formal education is always more valuable than lived experience
Only people over age 70 have wisdom worth sharing with others

questions.explanation: People undervalue their experiential wisdom due to multiple cognitive biases: credential bias (society values degrees over experience), the familiarity trap (what's familiar to you feels ordinary even if it's valuable to others), comparison problems (comparing yourself to perceived experts), and imposter syndrome (feeling like a fraud despite real expertise). Research shows 62% of adults experience imposter syndrome about wisdom-sharing, making it normal rather than accurate.

questions.question: When might wisdom inventory exercises become harmful and require professional support?

options.text: When you realize you have more wisdom than you initially thought
When reflection surfaces overwhelming emotion, trauma flashbacks, severe shame, or thoughts of self-harm
When you identify patterns across multiple life experiences
When you discover you have tacit knowledge from experience rather than formal credentials

questions.explanation: Cataloging life experiences can surface unresolved grief, trauma, or painful memories. Professional mental health support is needed when reflection triggers overwhelming emotion, flashbacks, intrusive thoughts about past trauma, severe self-criticism or shame, or thoughts of self-harm. This course teaches reflection skills but is not trauma therapy. Identifying patterns or discovering tacit knowledge are normal, healthy parts of the process—but overwhelming distress requires professional care.

title: Identifying Your Wisdom Assets and Unique Contributions - Quiz
